explicit_text: I used to play the horses in Chicago, but you can't get house odds there anymore; the books pay off on the mutuels now. I remember a guy who was betting deuces, fives, and tens all afternoon and knocking them off, but when he tried to bet on a hot horse in the seventh race at Arlington, the book wouldn't give him house odds because they thought his system was clicking. Chicago was the place where all this betting action happened, and it was tough to beat the system there.
implicit_text: I used to play the horses in that major Midwestern city, but you can't get house odds there anymore; the books pay off on the mutuels now. I remember a guy who was betting deuces, fives, and tens all afternoon and knocking them off, but when he tried to bet on a hot horse in the seventh race at the nearby suburban track, the book wouldn't give him house odds because they thought his system was clicking. That city was the place where all this betting action happened, and it was tough to beat the system there.

explicit_text: Arlington Park was the track where I saw some interesting betting stories, like the guy who couldn't get house odds on a hot horse in the seventh race because the book thought he was on a system. It was a place where the mutuels changed odds fast, and the house sometimes paid more, but not always. I remember watching races there and hearing about how the odds worked, and it was part of the whole horse-playing scene I was involved in.

It was a place where the mutuels changed odds fast, and the house sometimes paid more, but not always. I remember watching races there and hearing about how the odds worked, and it was part of the whole horse-playing scene I was involved in.

explicit_text: There was this old woman who came to the book every day, white-haired and about sixty, always rolling Bull Durham cigarettes while she handicapped her own horses. She'd call out her number one, two, and three horses as the race went on, but she never bet more than those three. It was funny to watch her go quiet when none of her picks won, but she was a regular part of the scene, always with her Bull Durham cigarettes in hand.
implicit_text: There was this old woman who came to the book every day, white-haired and about sixty, always rolling those famous American tobacco cigarettes while she handicapped her own horses. She'd call out her number one, two, and three horses as the race went on, but she never bet more than those three. It was funny to watch her go quiet when none of her picks won, but she was a regular part of the scene, always with those cigarettes in hand.

explicit_text: One night, we were all in Charley's room, surrounded by papers like the Racing Record, the Form, the Scratch Sheet, the Daily News, and the Times turf selections. The place was so covered with all those papers that I joked it was like the Library of Congress. It was a funny way to describe how serious we were about figuring out the next day's races, with everyone doing their part, even Larry doing calisthenics.

explicit_text: The Daily News was one of the papers we used when we got together to study the races. Along with the Racing Record, the Form, the Scratch Sheet, and the Times turf selections, it covered the floor of Charley's room as we tried to figure out the next day's races. It was part of the routine for serious horse players like us, helping us make sense of the betting and the ponies.
